stories.headline: Israel: We'll Halt Strikes In Iran, But Continue In Lebanon
stories.summary: Israel has halted strikes in Iran as part of a two-week ceasefire deal, but will continue operations against the Iran-backed Hezbollah militia in Lebanon. The US and Israel claim Lebanon is not included in the ceasefire agreement, which was reached after their war in Iran began on February 28. However, Iranian insistence was that Lebanon be included in the deal, and continued Israeli attacks in Lebanon could jeopardize the ceasefire. US representative JD Vance dismissed Iran's allegations of a ceasefire breach, stating their remarks "didn't make sense". The ceasefire agreement aims to halt Israeli military operations, but the situation in Lebanon remains uncertain. Oil tankers have been halted in the Hormuz Strait following Israeli strikes on Lebanon, adding to the regional tensions. The conflict began after negotiations over Iran's nuclear program.

stories.headline: Hezbollah says Israel's attacks on Lebanon affirms its right to a response
stories.summary: Hezbollah has stated that Israel's attacks on Lebanon affirm its right to respond, strengthening the group's determination to resist and confront the enemy. Israel confirmed the attack on a Hezbollah commander in Beirut, which was part of a wave of strikes across Lebanon that left at least 254 people dead. Hezbollah has fired rockets at northern Israel, as disagreements continue over whether Lebanon is part of the ceasefire. The attacks occurred despite a ceasefire agreement between the US and Iran being announced hours earlier. Wednesday's strikes marked Israel's biggest attack on Lebanon since the beginning of the recent war. Hezbollah maintains it has the right to respond to Israeli attacks, and the situation remains volatile with ongoing exchanges of fire between the two sides. The conflict has resulted in significant casualties and damage.

stories.headline: Middle East crisis live: strait of Hormuz appears to remain closed amid conflicting US and Iran briefings
stories.summary: The Strait of Hormuz remains closed amid conflicting US and Iran briefings. Iran claims Israel violated a ceasefire agreement by launching an attack on Lebanon, prompting Tehran to close the strait. The White House insists the strait must stay open, but Iran's Revolutionary Guards say shipping has stopped. A UK-led coalition of 40 countries has vowed to take action against the closure, with UK Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper accusing Iran of blockading the waterway to "hold the global economy hostage". The closure has caused confusion among shipowners, who are trying to determine if they can safely transit the vital waterway. The fragile ceasefire between the US and Iran is at risk of being disrupted by the ongoing tensions. Iran maintains control over shipping in the strait, despite international calls for it to be reopened.

stories.headline: Lebanon 'separate skirmish' and not part of Iran ceasefire deal, Trump tells US media
stories.summary: US President Donald Trump stated that the conflict in Lebanon is not part of the temporary ceasefire deal between the US and Iran, citing the presence of Hezbollah as the reason. Trump told PBS News that Lebanon will "get taken care of" and referred to the situation as a "separate skirmish." This contradicts Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi's claim that the ceasefire includes an end to the war in Lebanon. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu agrees with Trump that the truce does not cover Lebanon. The development comes as Israel continues to assault Lebanon, prompting Qatar to condemn the attacks as a "flagrant violation of international law." The situation has sparked concerns about the stability of the ceasefire, with Iran threatening to retaliate unless the attacks stop.

stories.headline: Google Pixel bests iPhone, Samsung in repairability report A new report calls out the top smartphone makers in the US for their poor device repairability scores, but Google...
stories.summary: Google Pixel smartphones have surpassed Apple's iPhone and Samsung's Galaxy devices in a repairability report. The Google Pixel 8 and Pixel 8 Pro earned a repairability score of 6/10, making them the most repair-friendly models among recent flagships. In contrast, all Apple iPhone 15 models received a low score of 4/10, indicating limited ease of repair. The report, titled "Failing the Fix 2026," was conducted by the US Public Interest Research Group (PIRG) Education Fund, which evaluated 105 devices from major US smartphone and laptop manufacturers. The study found that Apple and Samsung earned failing grades for smartphone repairability, while Motorola topped the rankings. The report highlights the need for manufacturers to improve device repairability, making it easier for consumers to fix their own devices and reduce electronic waste. The findings provide valuable insights for tech consumers and advocates.

stories.headline: Apple and Samsung Rank Worst in Phone Repairability, New Study Finds Apple and Samsung earn failing grades for smartphone repairability while Motorola tops rankings in comprehensive US PIRG study of 105 devices.
stories.summary: A recent study by the US PIRG Education Fund found that Apple and Samsung ranked worst in smartphone repairability. The "Failing the Fix 2026" report assessed 105 devices and gave Samsung a D grade and Apple a D- grade, placing them second-last and last respectively. Motorola topped the rankings, outperforming other major brands. According to the report, Samsung and Apple phones are more difficult to repair than those from other manufacturers, posing challenges for consumers in terms of cost, waste, and choice. The study highlights the need for more repairable devices, citing the environmental and economic benefits of easier repair options. The low repairability scores of Apple and Samsung raise concerns about electronic waste and consumer access to affordable repair services. The report aims to promote sustainability and consumer choice in the smartphone industry.

stories.headline: Google Chrome now has vertical tabs Google has added vertical tabs to Chrome, and reading mode has also been improved.
stories.summary: Google Chrome has introduced vertical tabs, offering an alternative to the traditional horizontal tab strip. This feature displays a list of open tabs on the left side of the screen, and users can switch to vertical tabs by right-clicking on any Chrome window and selecting "Show Tabs Vertically". Additionally, Google has improved its reading mode, now presenting it in a full-page interface. The updated reading mode transforms graphics-heavy pages into a clean, document-like view, unlike its previous side-panel version. Both features are being rolled out broadly, making them available to all users without the need to enable experimental flags. The updates aim to enhance the browsing experience, providing users with more flexibility and a more immersive reading environment. The new features are now available in the latest version of Google Chrome for desktop.

stories.headline: iPhone Air 2 and iPhone 18e Reportedly Launching Early Next Year Apple plans to release an updated iPhone Air and a lower-end iPhone 18e early next year, according to the latest word from Bloomberg's Mark Gurman.
stories.summary: Apple plans to release an updated iPhone Air, dubbed iPhone Air 2, and a lower-end iPhone 18e early next year. According to Bloomberg's Mark Gurman, these models will launch in spring 2027, alongside the standard iPhone 18. This release schedule marks a departure from Apple's usual annual iPhone calendar. The company is expected to adopt a split release strategy, unveiling some models in autumn and others in spring to spread demand and ease production pressure. The iPhone Air 2 and iPhone 18e will be part of this two-stage roadmap for the iPhone 18 line, with the base iPhone 18 variant delayed from fall 2026 to spring 2027. This change aims to manage production pressure and demand for Apple's iPhone lineup. The exact release dates and specifications of the new models have not been officially confirmed by Apple.

stories.headline: Leicester City face Championship relegation to League One after an independent commission rejected their appeal against a six-point deduction for PSR breaches.
stories.summary: Leicester City's appeal against a six-point deduction for breaching English Football League financial rules has been rejected by an independent commission. The sanction, imposed in February, remains in place, leaving the club 22nd in the Championship table, one point from safety with five matches remaining. The Premier League-appointed independent commission initially imposed the deduction, prompting Leicester to fall to 20th in the table. The club appealed on grounds including the commission's power to recommend the points deduction and the level of sanction. However, an independent appeal board has upheld the penalty, further complicating Leicester's battle against relegation to League One. The decision comes after Leicester's successful 2016 Premier League title triumph, and the club now faces a challenging fight to avoid dropping to the third tier.

stories.headline: Jason Day delivers a candid, no-nonsense reaction to Tiger Woods’ DUI arrest, offering a perspective many in golf haven’t openly shared.
stories.summary: Jason Day shared a candid reaction to Tiger Woods' DUI arrest, offering a perspective that many in golf haven't openly shared. Day's comments come after Woods' arrest, which has garnered significant attention. The golf community has been awaiting updates on the situation, with some expressing frustration over the lack of new information. Woods' arrest has also sparked a wave of memes and discussion about his impact on the sport. As a major champion, Day's thoughts on the matter carry weight, and his willingness to speak openly about the issue is notable. The incident has raised questions about Woods' personal life and its potential impact on his career, with many in the golf world watching to see how the situation unfolds. Day's comments add to the ongoing conversation about Woods and his place in the sport.

stories.headline: Tamim eyes quick restart of Dhaka Premier League amid stalemate
stories.summary: Tamim Iqbal is pushing for a quick restart of the Dhaka Premier League, which has been stalled due to a stalemate. The league has been at a standstill, and Tamim, a key figure in Bangladesh cricket, is eager to see the tournament resume. The Dhaka Premier League is a prominent domestic cricket competition in Bangladesh, and its suspension has affected the cricket calendar. Tamim's call for a swift restart aims to get the league back on track, ensuring that cricket activities in the country can proceed without further disruption. The stalemate has been ongoing, and a resolution is needed to revive the league and provide a platform for players to compete. Tamim's initiative is expected to prompt discussions among stakeholders to find a solution and restart the league.

stories.headline: Meet Yusra Mardini: The 17-year-old Olympian who helped save 20 lives at sea
stories.summary: Yusra Mardini, a 28-year-old Syrian former competition swimmer and refugee, gained international recognition for her bravery and athletic achievements. In 2015, at 17, she and her sister Sara fled Syria, boarding an overcrowded dinghy from Turkey to Greece. When the engine failed, Yusra and her sister helped save 20 lives by swimming and pushing the boat to shore. She later became a member of the first Refugee Olympic Athletes Team. Yusra was appointed the youngest-ever Goodwill Ambassador for the UNHCR in 2017 and now speaks globally on refugee issues, expanding her mission beyond swimming. She uses her platform to raise awareness about refugee issues and support those affected by conflict.

stories.headline: [The Hindu] Surprised that NCERT singled out three experts on book row: Alok Prasanna Kumar
stories.summary: The National Council of Educational Research and Training (NCERT) has been directed by the Centre to review all textbooks after the Supreme Court raised concerns over content in a Class 8 social science book. The controversy surrounds a chapter on corruption in the judiciary. The Supreme Court has asked the Centre and states to disassociate from three experts: Michael Danino, Alok Prasanna Kumar, and Suparna Diwakar. Alok Prasanna Kumar has expressed surprise that NCERT singled out these three experts. The government has constituted an expert committee, comprising a former apex court judge, to review the matter. The three experts, who were barred from offering their expertise, have sought a hearing. The row has led to a review of all NCERT textbooks, with the Centre taking action to address the Supreme Court's concerns. An expert committee will examine the content.

stories.headline: [Live Law] 'No Bias Can Be Attached To Justice Swarana Kanta Sharma' : CBI Opposes Kejriwal's Recusal Application
stories.summary: The Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI) has opposed an application by Arvind Kejriwal and other accused persons seeking the recusal of Justice Swarana Kanta Sharma from a Delhi High Court case. The case is related to the Delhi liquor excise policy. Solicitor General Tushar Mehta, representing the CBI, termed the allegations against Justice Sharma as "frivolous and contemptuous". Kejriwal had appeared in person before the High Court on April 6 and made submissions on his plea, stating he would argue the recusal application himself. The court has listed the case for hearing on April 13. Kejriwal maintained he was following procedure, as a petitioner in person is not allowed to file an application without a court order, and he had not yet issued a vakalatnama to any lawyer. The CBI's opposition will be considered during the next hearing.

stories.headline: [The Hindu] Pawan Khera accuses Assam CM Himanta of using the police to silence questions
stories.summary: Congress leader Pawan Khera accused Assam Chief Minister Himanta Biswa Sarma of using the state police to intimidate him and silence questions about alleged foreign assets and documents linked to the CM's wife. Khera alleged that Sarma is targeting him instead of responding to the opposition party's questions on multiple passports and foreign connections. The accusations come a day before the state polls on April 9. Assam Police investigated Khera in Delhi amid allegations of misinformation linked to Sarma. Khera claimed that the CM is trying to silence him through police intimidation rather than addressing the questions raised by the opposition party. The Congress leader recorded a video from an undisclosed location, questioning why Sarma wants to silence him. The allegations have sparked a controversy ahead of the state elections.

stories.headline: [Bar and Bench] Attending RSS Seminar doesn’t prove bias: CBI in Kejriwal plea for Justice Swarna Kanta Recusal
stories.summary: The Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI) has filed an affidavit opposing the applications of Arvind Kejriwal, Manish Sisodia, and four others seeking the recusal of Justice Swarna Kanta Sharma in the Delhi excise policy case. The six have cited Justice Sharma's alleged ideological association with the Akhil Bharat Adhivakta Parishad (ABAP), an affiliate of the Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS), as a reason for recusal. However, the CBI argues that attending an RSS seminar does not prove bias. The applicants had moved a plea citing a grave and reasonable apprehension of bias regarding the proceedings. The Delhi High Court will hear the matter on April 13. The plea seeks Justice Sharma's recusal from hearing the CBI's challenge against Kejriwal's discharge in the case. The CBI's opposition affidavit contends that attendance at a legal seminar does not imply bias.

stories.headline: Oil, Markets, and the World Saved from Annihilation Diplomacy triumphs over brinkmanship as the US-Iran ceasefire stabilises markets and geopolitics.
stories.summary: The US and Iran have agreed to a conditional two-week ceasefire deal, easing tensions and stabilizing markets. The agreement includes the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, a key waterway. Global oil prices have fallen sharply, by nearly 15%, and stock markets have jumped in response. The ceasefire reduces immediate geopolitical risk, causing traders to dial back rate hike expectations. Iran had previously rejected the US's ultimatum to reopen the Strait, demanding compensation for war damage instead. The deal comes after a period of escalating tensions, with Iran striking energy targets in Gulf neighbors, including Kuwait's oil headquarters. The agreement has brought relief to markets, but its implications for the US and Iran's relationship are still being assessed. The ceasefire is conditional, and its long-term impact on the region's geopolitics remains to be seen.

stories.headline: Did China Broker The US-Iran Ceasefire? Beijing’s Quiet Diplomacy Before Trump-Xi Meeting China is emerging as a key player in the US-Iran ceasefire, reportedly pushing Tehran to negotiate through backchannel diplomacy.
stories.summary: China played a key role in brokering the US-Iran ceasefire through backchannel diplomacy, reportedly pushing Tehran to negotiate. The move signals China's growing influence in global geopolitics, particularly with its strategic interests in the Middle East. As Iran's leading trade partner, China buys around 80% of Iranian oil, making stability in the region crucial. Beijing's quiet diplomacy aimed to avoid being drawn into America's conflict while securing its energy interests. China has previously attempted to play peacemaker in the Middle East, with limited success. The country's efforts to broker a ceasefire come ahead of a delayed visit from US President Trump to China, highlighting Beijing's increasing importance in global affairs. China's role in the ceasefire demonstrates its ability to navigate complex geopolitical situations to protect its interests.

stories.headline: Soybeans at the Crossroads: Trade Geopolitics, Biofuel Tailwinds, and a Market Searching for Direction Soybean futures have been structurally supported since the start of 2025, with buyers consistently stepping up after each rotation lower.
stories.summary: Soybean futures have been supported since 2025, with buyers stepping in after each decline. Stronger vegetable oil demand from new US biodiesel mandates and high crude oil prices have contributed to the support, while weak US export sales and expectations of larger 2026 soy acreage have capped gains. Chinese soybean demand is absent, but US ports remain active due to PNW corn exports. The US and Brazil are expected to compete strongly in the ag space, with a leader warning of a strong response from Brazil. New technologies, such as Vyconic Soybeans, are changing crop planning, offering growers more flexible options. Guides are also available to help manage soybean diseases, including soil testing and resistant varieties. Overall, the soybean market is searching for direction, influenced by trade geopolitics, biofuel demand, and shifting global supply dynamics.

stories.headline: Crisis is about danger and opportunity MY friend, newspaper columnist Edgardo Lacson, reacted to my April 2 column, “When Elephants Fight, They Step on the Ants,“ through a Viber message: “Partner, y
stories.summary: Newspaper columnist Edgardo Lacson responded to a column about crisis and opportunity, referencing a Chinese idiom "Xiao chong bu ke yu bing." The conversation started with a column titled "When Elephants Fight, They Step on the Ants," which discussed the impact of crisis on the general population. Former Vice President Kamala Harris had previously mentioned the concept of forming a strong circle in times of crisis, citing a viral video of elephants at the San Diego Zoo Safari Park. Lacson's response highlighted the thin line between accountability and opportunism during crises, as seen in a recent Senate hearing on the country's energy situation. The exchange emphasizes the importance of recognizing the pulse of the nation and responding strategically to crises, rather than hiding behind emotions or opportunism.

stories.headline: Stock Markets Today: Sensex, Nifty50 Surge as US-Iran Tensions Ease Indian markets on Dalal Street rallied sharply as easing tensions in the US-Iran conflict and stable oil prices boosted sentiment.
stories.summary: Indian stock markets rallied sharply as US-Iran tensions eased, with the Sensex surging 2,989 points to 77,605.40 and the Nifty 50 jumping 890 points to 24,014. The benchmark indices reacted positively to the announcement of a US-Iran ceasefire, which ended hostilities and caused a sharp drop in crude oil prices. The Sensex soared 2,840 points or 3.8% to 77,456.11, while the Nifty 50 rallied 838 points. The market showed bullish sentiment, with equity benchmark indices ending nearly 4% higher on Wednesday. The rally was driven by positive global market sentiment and hopes for de-escalation in the West Asia conflict. Asian markets, including South Korea and Japan, also experienced substantial gains. The ceasefire agreement and stable oil prices boosted investor sentiment, leading to a significant surge in the Indian stock markets.

stories.headline: Sensex and Nifty Rally as US-Iran Ceasefire Boosts Global Markets Indian stock markets experienced a significant rally following the announcement of a US-Iran ceasefire, coupled with a drop in crude oil prices.
stories.summary: Indian stock markets rallied significantly after the US-Iran ceasefire announcement, easing geopolitical tensions and causing a sharp drop in crude oil prices. The Sensex and Nifty surged, with the Sensex gaining 2,919 points and the Nifty rising 868 points. Brent crude oil dropped over 5% to around $103 per barrel, while WTI crude plunged even more sharply. This decline is significant for India, which imports around 85% of its crude oil needs. The ceasefire has lifted risk appetite and boosted market sentiment, with Asian stock markets also spiking on the news. The reopening of the Strait of Hormuz is expected to further stabilize oil prices, supporting the market rally. The two-week ceasefire has dramatically altered the near-term market scenario, turning it bullish.

stories.headline: Market Cheers Ceasefire In Biggest Rally This Year So Far, Sensex Jumps Nearly 2,950 Points Indian stock markets rallied for a fifth straight session on April 8, with the Sensex rising 2,946 points and Nifty gaining 3.78%, driven by easing global tensions
stories.summary: Indian stock markets rallied for a fifth straight session on April 8, driven by easing global tensions and steady domestic policy signals. The Sensex surged 3.95% to close at 77,562.90, rising 2,946.32 points, while the Nifty gained 3.78% to end at 23,997.35, adding 873.70 points. This marked the biggest rally this year so far, with both indices posting strong gains. The positive momentum was fueled by reduced global tensions and consistent domestic policy signals. The Sensex and Nifty have now advanced for five consecutive sessions, with the latest gains reflecting improved investor sentiment. The rally indicates a significant turnaround in the market, with investors responding positively to the easing of global tensions and steady domestic policy environment.

stories.headline: The stock market on Dalal Street had a strong day, with Sensex rising by 2,900 points and Nifty surpassing the 24,000 mark. This was driven by positive global sentiments following the US-Iran ceasefire announcement.
stories.summary: The Indian stock market experienced a significant surge, with the Sensex rising by 2,900 points and the Nifty surpassing the 24,000 mark. This uptrend was driven by positive global sentiments following the US-Iran ceasefire announcement. The rally was supported by improving global conditions, including a sharp correction in crude oil prices and easing geopolitical tensions. Market breadth remained strong, with over 3,600 stocks advancing against fewer than 450 declines. The ceasefire deal between the US and Iran boosted investor confidence, leading to a sharp increase in domestic equities. The Nifty50 index briefly crossed the 24,000 mark intraday, indicating a strong risk-on sentiment in the market. The overall trend suggests a positive outlook for the Indian stock market, driven by favorable global developments and easing tensions. The market's upward momentum is expected to continue, driven by improving investor sentiment.

stories.headline: Meta Unveils New A.I. Model, Its First From the Superintelligence Lab
stories.summary: Meta Platforms Inc. has unveiled its new flagship artificial intelligence model, Muse Spark, developed by Meta Superintelligence Labs. This is the first model released by the lab, which was established as part of the company's revamped AI efforts. Muse Spark, initially code-named Avocado, is the first in a new series of models and will be available on the Meta AI app and website, initially only in the US. The model's release marks a significant milestone for Meta's AI organization, which has been overhauled to keep pace with rivals. The launch of Muse Spark comes after Mark Zuckerberg invested billions in the company's AI efforts, signaling a major push into the field of artificial intelligence. The model is expected to power various AI applications and services offered by Meta.

stories.headline: Microsoft’s executive shake-up continues as developer division chief resigns
stories.summary: Microsoft's developer division chief, Julia Liuson, is resigning after 34 years at the company. She led the developer business for 12 years, overseeing a period of increased focus on open source projects and the $7.5 billion acquisition of GitHub. Liuson will remain in her role until the end of June before transitioning to an advisory position. Her departure is part of an executive shake-up at Microsoft. Liuson spent over three decades at the software giant, playing a key role in shaping its developer division. Her resignation follows other significant changes at the company, including the appointment of a new chief accessibility officer, Neil Barnett, and a new chief, Nanda Ramachandran. Liuson's experience and leadership will be retained in an advisory capacity after her resignation takes effect.

stories.headline: Amid the 'SaaSpocalypse,' CIOs and CTOs take a harder line with their vendors
stories.summary: CIOs and CTOs are taking a harder line with their SaaS vendors amid the "SaaSpocalypse," a term used to describe the fundamental rebalancing of power between software vendors and their corporate customers. As investors debate the threat AI poses to enterprise software companies, IT leaders are renegotiating, consolidating, and canceling SaaS contracts. This shift is driven by the changing needs of enterprise technology leaders, who are becoming increasingly demanding. The "SaaSpocalypse" is characterized by a more aggressive approach to vendor relationships, with CIOs and CTOs seeking better deals and more flexible contracts. Major vendors, including Salesforce and Intuit, are likely to be impacted by this trend, as corporate customers assert their newfound bargaining power. The result is a significant change in the dynamics between software vendors and their customers.

stories.headline: Stock Market Rebound: 3 Top AI Bargains to Snap Up Now
stories.summary: The stock market is experiencing a rebound, presenting opportunities to invest in artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure stocks. Three top AI bargain stocks are available to snap up now, offering fantastic growth prospects in the short and long term. These stocks provide computing power and are expected to perform well in 2026. Investing in AI infrastructure can uncover strategic market opportunities, with tools like InvestingPro offering real-time quotes, charts, and news to aid in research. Meanwhile, global stock markets have been impacted by US President Donald Trump's sweeping tariffs on imports, but the AI sector remains a promising area for investment. Investors can research and compare the performance of top dividend stocks for long-term growth and steady income, utilizing resources like Investing.com and The Irish Sun for latest news and market analysis.

stories.headline: Marc Andreessen sketches AGI pricing for advanced AI systems like OpenClaw
stories.summary: Marc Andreessen has outlined a tiered pricing vision for artificial general intelligence (AGI) systems, including paid large language models (LLMs) like Claude and OpenAI. This pricing model is intended for advanced AI systems, such as OpenClaw. Andreessen's vision comes as companies are making rapid progress towards achieving AGI, with some predicting it will be realized within the next few years. The development of AGI has sparked discussions about responsible AI behavior and safety, with some experts expressing concerns about the leadership of companies like OpenAI. Meanwhile, AI systems are achieving significant milestones, such as surpassing human performance on certain benchmarks, highlighting the rapid advancement of the field. Andreessen's pricing vision suggests that AGI systems will be offered at varying price points, potentially making them more accessible to a wider range of users.

stories.headline: An AI threat looms, and we are not prepared | Juhyun Nam
stories.summary: An AI threat is looming, and experts warn that the government is not prepared to handle a potential crisis. According to Juhyun Nam, legal authority is needed to allow the government to shut down a dangerous AI system immediately in the event of a crisis. The current lack of preparedness and regulatory framework poses a significant risk. As AI technology continues to advance, the need for a proactive approach to mitigate potential threats is becoming increasingly urgent. The development of AI systems that can cause harm requires a corresponding development of laws and regulations to prevent and respond to AI-related crises. The government must take steps to establish a framework for addressing AI threats and ensuring public safety. This includes granting the necessary legal authority to shut down dangerous AI systems.

stories.headline: The leaders of the world’s leading artificial intelligence (AI) companies — OpenAI, Google Deepmind, Anthropic — signed a letter in 2023 warning of the existent
stories.summary: Leaders of top artificial intelligence companies, including OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and Anthropic, have issued warnings about the risks of advanced AI systems. In 2023, they signed a letter cautioning about the dangers of AI, and recent statements have reiterated these concerns. Google DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis has warned about the rapid approach of artificial general intelligence, while Anthropic's CEO has expressed discomfort about the concentration of power among a few AI leaders. Over 330 employees from OpenAI and Google have also signed a solidarity letter, "We Will Not Be Divided," highlighting industry concerns. The warnings emphasize the potential risks of extinction and the need for responsible development and regulation of AI technology. Industry leaders and employees are calling for caution and cooperation to mitigate the potential dangers of advanced AI systems.

stories.headline: Paid LLMs such as Claude and OpenAI for open-source AI systems
stories.summary: Marc Andreessen has outlined a tiered pricing vision for artificial general intelligence (AGI). Meanwhile, paid LLMs such as Claude and OpenAI are being used for open-source AI systems. The new GLM-5.1 model can work autonomously for up to eight hours on a single task, marking a shift from vibe coding to agentic engineering. OpenAI has announced $24B ARR, but ChatGPT growth has stalled, and there have been personnel changes. Additionally, Claude Pro and Max subscribers can no longer use their credits through third-party frameworks like OpenClaw due to the strain it puts on systems. Anthropic has cited this as the reason for ending support for OpenClaw, effective April 4. These developments indicate significant changes in the AI landscape, with companies adapting to new demands and limitations. The shift towards autonomous models and tiered pricing may shape the future of AI development.
